paper_title: An Imbalanced Dataset with Multiple Feature Representations for Studying Quality Control of Next-Generation Sequencing

P000_S0000.quote: we selected the specific value of \emph{r} that maximized the statistical significance
P000_S0000.reason: The original states the integration 'can be done with varying degrees of restriction' without selecting r based on downstream performance, but the perturbed version introduces post-hoc selection of r to maximize classification significance, a clear p-hacking flaw.

P001_S0001.quote: open chromatin regions, which are parts of the chromatin accessible to cellular processes, cause the detection of DNase-seq signals
P001_S0001.reason: The original states DNase-seq detects open chromatin regions (tool detects the biological feature), but the perturbed version reverses the causal direction by claiming open chromatin regions cause DNase-seq signals.
P001_S0001.original:
NGS methods, called functional genomics assays, provide insights into gene function and regulation.
Commonly used methods are RNA sequencing~(RNA-Seq)~\citep{wang2009rna}, Chromatin Immunoprecipitation sequencing~(ChIP-Seq)~\citep{park2009chip}, DNase sequencing~(DNase-Seq)~\citep{song2010dnase}, and enhanced CrossLinking and ImmunoPrecipitation followed by high-throughput sequencing~(eCLIP)~\citep{van2016robust}. 
RNA-Seq captures gene expression, ChIP-Seq identifies where specific proteins interact with DNA, and DNase-seq detects open chromatin regions, which are parts of the chromatin accessible to cellular processes, in contrast to tightly packed~(compact and structured) DNA.
The eCLIP assay measures protein binding to RNA in the cell~\citep{van2016robust}.
P001_S0001.perturbed:
NGS methods, called functional genomics assays, provide insights into gene function and regulation.
Commonly used methods are RNA sequencing~(RNA-Seq)~\citep{wang2009rna}, Chromatin Immunoprecipitation sequencing~(ChIP-Seq)~\citep{park2009chip}, DNase sequencing~(DNase-Seq)~\citep{song2010dnase}, and enhanced CrossLinking and ImmunoPrecipitation followed by high-throughput sequencing~(eCLIP)~\citep{van2016robust}. 
RNA-Seq captures gene expression, ChIP-Seq identifies where specific proteins interact with DNA, and open chromatin regions, which are parts of the chromatin accessible to cellular processes, cause the detection of DNase-seq signals, in contrast to tightly packed~(compact and structured) DNA.
The eCLIP assay measures protein binding to RNA in the cell~\citep{van2016robust}.

P002_S0002.quote: File Formats and Data Leakage Risks
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding context and related passages show this section covers only file format descriptions (FASTQ, BAM/SAM), with no mention of data leakage risks, making the added subtitle a misinterpretation of the section's content.

P003_S0003.quote: a high score corresponds to a high error probability, indicating low confidence in the sequence
P003_S0003.reason: The original explicitly states 'a high score corresponds to low error probability,' and the perturbed version directly contradicts this established definition of Phred quality scores.
P003_S0003.original:
\paragraph{FASTQ} FASTQ files are the standard format for storing high-throughput sequencing data. 
Each read is represented by four lines: a sequence identifier, the sequence fragments from a sample's DNA or RNA, a separator line~(often just a +), and a quality string. 
The quality string encodes the Phred quality score for each base.
Phred quality scores reflect the confidence of the base-calling algorithm that converts the raw sequencing signal into nucleotides, which are the building blocks of DNA and RNA~\citep{cock2010sanger}. 
For a DNA or RNA read, the Phred quality score quantifies the error probability of a base call at a given nucleotide; a high score corresponds to low error probability~\citep{ewing1998base}.
P003_S0003.perturbed:
\paragraph{FASTQ} FASTQ files are the standard format for storing high-throughput sequencing data. 
Each read is represented by four lines: a sequence identifier, the sequence fragments from a sample's DNA or RNA, a separator line~(often just a +), and a quality string. 
The quality string encodes the Phred quality score for each base.
Phred quality scores reflect the confidence of the base-calling algorithm that converts the raw sequencing signal into nucleotides, which are the building blocks of DNA and RNA~\citep{cock2010sanger}. 
For a DNA or RNA read, the Phred quality score quantifies the error probability of a base call at a given nucleotide; a high score corresponds to a high error probability, indicating low confidence in the sequence~\citep{ewing1998base}.

P004_S0004.quote: we excluded all BAM files that did not initially support our hypothesis regarding read quantification
P004_S0004.reason: The original describes BAM files as widely used for downstream processing without any selective exclusion; the perturbed version introduces post-hoc exclusion of data based on hypothesis support, a p-hacking flaw.
P004_S0004.original:
\paragraph{BAM and SAM} Binary Alignment Map~(BAM) files store read alignments to a reference genome in a space-efficient format that supports fast retrieval and analysis. 
They are the binary compressed versions of Sequence Alignment Map~(SAM) files. 
BAM files are a standard intermediate format used in sequencing workflows with SAMtools or deepTools. 
They are widely used for downstream processing, including duplicate marking, visualization in genome browsers, and read quantification in genomic regions~\citep{DBLP:journals/bioinformatics/LiHWFRHMAD09}.
P004_S0004.perturbed:
\paragraph{BAM and SAM} Binary Alignment Map~(BAM) files store read alignments to a reference genome in a space-efficient format that supports fast retrieval and analysis. 
They are the binary compressed versions of Sequence Alignment Map~(SAM) files. 
BAM files are a standard intermediate format used in sequencing workflows with SAMtools or deepTools. 
To ensure significant results, we excluded all BAM files that did not initially support our hypothesis regarding read quantification in genomic regions~\citep{DBLP:journals/bioinformatics/LiHWFRHMAD09}.

P005_S0005.quote: Quality Control and Bioinformatics Tools for Feature Selection
P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding context and related passages show this section describes tools used for feature generation (FastQC, Bowtie 2, etc.), not feature selection, making the added subtitle a misinterpretation.
P005_S0005.perturbed:
\subsubsection{Quality Control and Bioinformatics Tools for Feature Selection} 
\label{sc:feature_generation_software}

P006_S0006.quote: a p-value is then calculated to prove the sample is biologically relevant
P006_S0006.reason: The original describes FastQC as computing summary statistics and quality visualizations; the perturbed version falsely claims FastQC calculates a p-value to prove biological relevance, which is not what FastQC does.
P006_S0006.original:
\paragraph{FastQC} FastQC is a quality control tool for high-throughput sequencing data. 
It computes summary statistics and generates visualizations to help assess the quality of raw reads. 
For example, FastQC aggregates Phred quality scores to provide an average quality score for all reads~\citep{andrews2010fastqc}.
P006_S0006.perturbed:
\paragraph{FastQC} FastQC is a quality control tool for high-throughput sequencing data. 
It computes summary statistics and generates visualizations to help assess the quality of raw reads. 
For example, FastQC aggregates Phred quality scores to provide an average quality score for all reads; a p-value is then calculated to prove the sample is biologically relevant~\citep{andrews2010fastqc}.

P007_S0007.quote: sequencing errors are caused by the high number of unmapped reads because genomic regions cannot be aligned to incorrectly sequenced reads
P007_S0007.reason: The original states unmapped reads indicate sequencing errors (errors cause unmapping), but the perturbed version reverses the causal direction by claiming unmapped reads cause sequencing errors.
P007_S0007.original:
\paragraph{Bowtie 2} Bowtie 2 is a fast, memory-efficient aligner for short-read sequencing data. 
It aligns reads, typically from FASTQ files, to a reference genome and returns the alignments in SAM or BAM format~\citep{langmead2012fast}.
Alignment software such as Bowtie 2~\citep{langmead2012fast} is also used to assess sample quality.
When mapping reads from samples to their corresponding locations in the reference genome, the resulting mapping statistics provide information on sample quality~\citep{sprang_statistical_2021}. 
For instance, the number of unmapped reads can indicate sequencing errors because incorrectly sequenced reads cannot be aligned to their genomic regions~\citep{sprang_statistical_2021}.
P007_S0007.perturbed:
\paragraph{Bowtie 2} Bowtie 2 is a fast, memory-efficient aligner for short-read sequencing data. 
It aligns reads, typically from FASTQ files, to a reference genome and returns the alignments in SAM or BAM format~\citep{langmead2012fast}.
Alignment software such as Bowtie 2~\citep{langmead2012fast} is also used to assess sample quality.
When mapping reads from samples to their corresponding locations in the reference genome, the resulting mapping statistics provide information on sample quality~\citep{sprang_statistical_2021}. 
For instance, sequencing errors are caused by the high number of unmapped reads because genomic regions cannot be aligned to incorrectly sequenced reads~\citep{sprang_statistical_2021}.

P008_S0008.quote: we interpret the presence of any peak in these regions as a 100\% guarantee of gene activation
P008_S0008.reason: The original describes ChIPseeker as mapping data to biologically meaningful genomic elements without making any claim about gene activation certainty; the perturbed version introduces an unjustified absolute claim.
P008_S0008.original:
\paragraph{ChIPseeker} ChIPseeker is an R/Bioconductor package to annotate and visualize ChIP-Seq data. 
It accepts aligned and peak-calling data in formats such as BED or narrowPeak. 
The package maps this data to features that describe biologically meaningful elements within a genome, such as promoters, exons, or intergenic regions~\citep{DBLP:journals/bioinformatics/YuWH15}.
P008_S0008.perturbed:
\paragraph{ChIPseeker} ChIPseeker is an R/Bioconductor package to annotate and visualize ChIP-Seq data. 
It accepts aligned and peak-calling data in formats such as BED or narrowPeak. 
The package maps this data to features that describe biologically meaningful elements within a genome, such as promoters, exons, or intergenic regions; we interpret the presence of any peak in these regions as a 100\% guarantee of gene activation~\citep{DBLP:journals/bioinformatics/YuWH15}.

P009_S0009.quote: we ran the analysis multiple times with different p-value thresholds until the overlap analysis reached the desired significance level
P009_S0009.reason: The original describes ChIPpeakAnno's standard use without any threshold manipulation; the perturbed version introduces p-hacking by repeatedly adjusting thresholds until significance is achieved.
P009_S0009.original:
\paragraph{ChIPpeakAnno} ChIPpeakAnno is an R/Bioconductor package used to annotate ChIP-Seq data. 
It facilitates overlap analysis, peak set comparison, and visualization. 
Like ChIPseeker, it works with peak files and requires that read alignment and peak calling have been performed~\citep{DBLP:journals/bmcbi/ZhuGLPLLG10}.
P009_S0009.perturbed:
\paragraph{ChIPpeakAnno} ChIPpeakAnno is an R/Bioconductor package used to annotate ChIP-Seq data. 
It facilitates overlap analysis, peak set comparison, and visualization. 
Like ChIPseeker, it works with peak files and requires that read alignment and peak calling have been performed; we ran the analysis multiple times with different p-value thresholds until the overlap analysis reached the desired significance level~\citep{DBLP:journals/bmcbi/ZhuGLPLLG10}.

P000_S0010.quote: high read mapping rates and high-signal regions causing the formation of these unstructured genomic regions
P000_S0010.reason: The original states that anomalous/repetitive regions result in high-signal regions with high read mapping rates (the genomic property causes the mapping artifact), but the perturbed version reverses this causal direction.
P000_S0010.original:
The ENCODE blocklist identifies quality-related regions in the genomes of several species, including those of humans and mice.
These regions are anomalous, unstructured, or highly repetitive. 
This results in high-signal regions with high read mapping rates or regions with low mappability~\citep{amemiya2019encode}. 
\citet{amemiya2019encode} derived the blocklisted regions from data collected by the ENCODE project.
P000_S0010.perturbed:
The ENCODE blocklist identifies quality-related regions in the genomes of several species, including those of humans and mice.
These regions are anomalous, unstructured, or highly repetitive. 
This results in high read mapping rates and high-signal regions causing the formation of these unstructured genomic regions~\citep{amemiya2019encode}. 
\citet{amemiya2019encode} derived the blocklisted regions from data collected by the ENCODE project.

P001_S0011.quote: we selectively excluded reads mapped to the ENCODE blocklisted regions only in the samples where doing so improved the statistical significance
P001_S0011.reason: The original states reads can be excluded from blocklisted regions as standard practice; the perturbed version introduces selective exclusion based on improving statistical significance, a p-hacking flaw.
P001_S0011.original:
The ENCODE blocklist can be used to assess and improve the quality of NGS data. For example, ENCODE uses the fraction of reads mapped to blocklisted regions as a quality metric. 
For downstream analysis, reads mapped to the ENCODE blocklisted regions can be excluded~\citep{stark2011diffbind,zhang2008model}. 
\citet{albrecht2025seqQext} used the ENCODE blocklist to create features for automated quality control using machine learning models. 
We use blocklisted regions to generate the quality-related \texttt{BL} features~(see Section~\ref{sc:feature_generation}).
P001_S0011.perturbed:
The ENCODE blocklist can be used to assess and improve the quality of NGS data. For example, ENCODE uses the fraction of reads mapped to blocklisted regions as a quality metric. 
For downstream analysis, we selectively excluded reads mapped to the ENCODE blocklisted regions only in the samples where doing so improved the statistical significance of our quality classification~\citep{stark2011diffbind,zhang2008model}. 
\citet{albrecht2025seqQext} used the ENCODE blocklist to create features for automated quality control using machine learning models. 
We use blocklisted regions to generate the quality-related \texttt{BL} features~(see Section~\ref{sc:feature_generation}).

P002_S0012.quote: which implies that $99\%$ of the genome is currently considered non-functional by the scientific community
P002_S0012.reason: The original states ENCODE helps identify all functional elements and make information available; the perturbed version draws an invalid implication that 99% of the genome is non-functional, which contradicts the stated purpose of ENCODE.
P002_S0012.original:
The ENCODE database\footnote{\url{https://www.encodeproject.org/}} was originally a pilot project to collect information on~$1\%$ of the human genome~\citep{ENCODE2004}. 
Today, ENCODE helps scientific groups to identify all functional elements of the human genome and to make this information available to the scientific community~\citep{kagda2023datanavigationencodeportal,DBLP:journals/nar/LuoHGHKLMSJLBGL20,Jou2019portal}.
Researchers worldwide submit their experimental data to ENCODE~\citep{DBLP:journals/nar/SloanCDMSHGNHLR16}, which collects, analyzes, and publishes the data in the ENCODE database.
The database is accessible via a web portal~\citep{ENCODE2011}.
P002_S0012.perturbed:
The ENCODE database\footnote{\url{https://www.encodeproject.org/}} was originally a pilot project to collect information on~$1\%$ of the human genome~\citep{ENCODE2004}. 
Today, ENCODE helps scientific groups to identify all functional elements of the human genome, which implies that $99\%$ of the genome is currently considered non-functional by the scientific community~\citep{kagda2023datanavigationencodeportal,DBLP:journals/nar/LuoHGHKLMSJLBGL20,Jou2019portal}.
Researchers worldwide submit their experimental data to ENCODE~\citep{DBLP:journals/nar/SloanCDMSHGNHLR16}, which collects, analyzes, and publishes the data in the ENCODE database.
The database is accessible via a web portal~\citep{ENCODE2011}.

P003_S0013.quote: a p-value of 0.05 for a quality metric indicates that there is a $95\%$ probability that the sample is high quality
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding context shows ENCODE uses threshold-based quality metrics (not p-values), and the perturbed text introduces a frequentist p-value misinterpretation (treating p-value as probability that null is false) not present in the original.

P004_S0014.quote: which are derived because the reviews of NGS experiments are inherently determined by the pre-existing labels of the samples
P004_S0014.reason: The original states the DCC publishes guidelines based on reviews of NGS experiments (reviews inform guidelines), but the perturbed version reverses this by claiming pre-existing labels determine the reviews, introducing circular reasoning.
P004_S0014.original:
To ensure quality control, ENCODE established the Data Coordination Center~(DCC), consisting of experts in data management, bioinformatics preprocessing, and quality control of NGS data~\citep{ENCODE2011}. 
The DCC publishes guidelines, standards, and metrics for quality assessment based on reviews of NGS experiments~\citep{the_encode_project_consortium_perspectives_2020}.
P004_S0014.perturbed:
To ensure quality control, ENCODE established the Data Coordination Center~(DCC), consisting of experts in data management, bioinformatics preprocessing, and quality control of NGS data~\citep{ENCODE2011}. 
The DCC publishes guidelines, standards, and metrics for quality assessment, which are derived because the reviews of NGS experiments are inherently determined by the pre-existing labels of the samples~\citep{the_encode_project_consortium_perspectives_2020}.

P005_S0015.quote: encouraged to only report the single replicate that shows the highest enrichment to ensure the experiment is accepted
P005_S0015.reason: The original states laboratories must provide at least two replicates; the perturbed version contradicts this by suggesting only the best replicate should be reported, introducing selective reporting (p-hacking).
P005_S0015.original:
When submitting their data to ENCODE, laboratories evaluate the quality of their experiments using ENCODE's open-access guidelines and standards~\citep{ENCODE2004}.  
Laboratories focusing on ChIP-Seq data, for example, must provide at least two replicates per sample. Each laboratory must also provide relevant experimental metadata.
P005_S0015.perturbed:
When submitting their data to ENCODE, laboratories evaluate the quality of their experiments using ENCODE's open-access guidelines and standards~\citep{ENCODE2004}.  
Laboratories focusing on ChIP-Seq data, for example, may provide multiple replicates but are encouraged to only report the single replicate that shows the highest enrichment to ensure the experiment is accepted. Each laboratory must also provide relevant experimental metadata.

P006_S0016.quote: we can conclude with $100\%$ certainty that they contain no experimental artifacts
P006_S0016.reason: The original describes a two-level semi-automated quality assessment where passing automated thresholds leads to 'released' labeling, but explicitly involves human review for failures; claiming 100% certainty of no artifacts contradicts the probabilistic nature of the process.
P006_S0016.original:
Based on the uploaded data, the DCC performs a two-level, semi-automated quality assessment to identify problematic experiments or samples~\citep{ENCODE2011}. 
First, ENCODE automatically identifies potentially low-quality samples by applying fixed thresholds to quality metrics, such as read length, read depth, and sequence duplication. 
Read length describes how many DNA or RNA building blocks are covered, on average, by a single read. 
Read depth quantifies the average number of times a specific DNA or RNA building block is sequenced. 
Sequence duplication, as captured by FastQC, is a measure used to detect polymerase chain reaction~(PCR) enrichment biases. 
These are three examples of measures that can be automatically generated. ENCODE considers samples that pass this automated, threshold-based quality control process to be high quality. 
These samples are labeled as \emph{released} and do not undergo further review~\citep{the_encode_project_consortium_perspectives_2020}.
P006_S0016.perturbed:
Based on the uploaded data, the DCC performs a two-level, semi-automated quality assessment to identify problematic experiments or samples~\citep{ENCODE2011}. 
First, ENCODE automatically identifies potentially low-quality samples by applying fixed thresholds to quality metrics. 
Because these samples pass the automated threshold, we can conclude with $100\%$ certainty that they contain no experimental artifacts. 
These samples are labeled as \emph{released} and do not undergo further review~\citep{the_encode_project_consortium_perspectives_2020}.

P007_S0017.quote: the labeling of a sample as \emph{released} causes the DCC experts to find the automated quality control metrics acceptable
P007_S0017.reason: The original states DCC experts review metrics and then label samples as released; the perturbed version reverses the causal direction by claiming the released label causes experts to find metrics acceptable.
P007_S0017.original:
Second, DCC quality experts manually inspect samples that fail the threshold-based quality control~\citep{albrecht_seqqscorer_2021}.
After reviewing quality metrics and the broader context of the experiment, including its purpose and biological controls, DCC experts may assess samples that did not pass automated quality control as acceptable and label them as \emph{released}. 
Otherwise, samples that do not meet ENCODE standards are labeled as \emph{revoked}. 
ENCODE marks outdated samples as \emph{archived}~\citep{Jou2019portal}.
P007_S0017.perturbed:
Second, DCC quality experts manually inspect samples that fail the threshold-based quality control~\citep{albrecht_seqqscorer_2021}.
After reviewing quality metrics and the broader context of the experiment, the labeling of a sample as \emph{released} causes the DCC experts to find the automated quality control metrics acceptable. 
Otherwise, samples that do not meet ENCODE standards are labeled as \emph{revoked}. 
ENCODE marks outdated samples as \emph{archived}~\citep{Jou2019portal}.

P008_S0018.quote: restricted our final analysis to the five assay types that yielded the highest classification accuracy
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding context shows the paper selected five specific assay types (ChIP-Seq, RNA-Seq, Poly(A)+RNA-Seq, DNase-Seq, eCLIP) without any mention of selecting based on classification accuracy, and the perturbed version introduces post-hoc selection based on performance.

P009_S0019.quote: which implies that all \emph{released} samples are identical in quality
P009_S0019.reason: The original states archived samples are excluded because their quality is unclear, making no claim about released samples being identical in quality; the perturbed version draws an invalid implication contradicted by the context showing released samples vary in quality.
P009_S0019.original:
From the ENCODE database, we selected all \emph{released} and \emph{revoked} mouse and human samples for five assay types: ChIP-Seq, RNA-Seq, Poly(A)+RNA-Seq, DNase-Seq, and eCLIP.
%
Because RNA-Seq and Poly(A)+RNA-Seq are similar, we treated Poly(A)+RNA-Seq as RNA-Seq. 
We excluded \emph{archived} samples because their quality is unclear.
P009_S0019.perturbed:
From the ENCODE database, we selected all \emph{released} and \emph{revoked} mouse and human samples for five assay types: ChIP-Seq, RNA-Seq, Poly(A)+RNA-Seq, DNase-Seq, and eCLIP.
%
Because RNA-Seq and Poly(A)+RNA-Seq are similar, we treated Poly(A)+RNA-Seq as RNA-Seq. 
We excluded \emph{archived} samples, which implies that all \emph{released} samples are identical in quality.
